did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for diagnosing melanoma skin lesions using supervised and deep learning algorithms. Specifically, it employed Deep Learning based Neural Networks (DLNN), Hybrid Adaboost-Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS), and Hybrid-AdaBoost algorithms for classification after feature extraction from dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for melanoma. It details the development of novel algorithms (Normalized Otsu’s Segmentation, DLNN, Hybrid Adaboost-SVM, ANFIS), describes the methodology including image acquisition from 992 dermoscopy images, feature extraction, and classification. It reports specific results, including a 93% classification accuracy, and validates the proposed system, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Novel Approaches for Diagnosing Melanoma Skin Lesions Through Supervised and Deep Learning Algorithms
brief_summary: This study proposes a Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for classifying melanoma skin lesions using dermoscopy images. The methodology involves preprocessing with CLAHE and median filtering, followed by lesion segmentation using a novel Normalized Otsu’s Segmentation (NOS) algorithm. Fifteen statistical and shape features were extracted from the segmented images and fed into various machine learning classifiers, including Deep Learning Neural Networks (DLNN), Hybrid Adaboost-SVM, Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS). The system was validated on a dataset of 992 dermoscopy images collected from various repositories. The proposed CAD system achieved a high classification accuracy of 93%, with DLNN and Hybrid-AdaBoost algorithms performing best among the tested methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system. The study focuses on diagnosing malignant melanoma using dermoscopy images. The dataset includes melanoma images collected from various repositories.
Used as the negative class or comparator to melanoma in the binary classification task. The dataset comprised 992 images including both melanoma and other benign skin lesion images.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (malignant melanoma vs. benign skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system to classify dermoscopy images into two discrete classes: malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions. The study employs supervised and deep learning algorithms (DLNN, Hybrid Adaboost-SVM, ANN, ANFIS, Hybrid-AdaBoost) to predict these diagnostic categories based on extracted features. The primary outcome metric is classification accuracy (93%), confirming the task is classification rather than regression.

datasets_used.name: Aggregated Dermoscopy Image Collection
datasets_used.description: A collection of 992 dermoscopy images acquired from various online repositories, including the BC Cancer Agency Skin Cancer Atlas, American Cancer Society, DermNet NZ, and Meddean Luc.edu. The images were captured using a Dermatoscope and digital cameras (Nikon D3 or Nikon D1x with Nikkor 2.8/105 mm micro lens).

use.training: Used for developing and training the Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system, including feature extraction and classifier evolution (DLNN, Hybrid Adaboost-SVM, ANN, ANFIS, Hybrid-AdaBoost).
use.validation: Used for validation of the proposed system; statistical validation was performed using Chi-square distribution with 5% level of significance.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification accuracy of the proposed algorithms.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Statistical validation of the proposed system.

statistical_methods_used.method: Normalization based on mean and standard deviation
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Feature normalization before input to classifiers.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference between the proposed CAD system and existing methods (implied by the use of Chi-square for validation).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference between the proposed CAD system and existing methods.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical validation was performed using Chi-square distribution with a 5% level of significance.

Aggregated Dermoscopy Image Collection (.notes: The proposed CAD system achieved an overall classification accuracy of 93%. DLNN provided the highest accuracy among the tested algorithms. The study also reported a Kappa value of 0.856 for the proposed system. Sensitivity and Specificity values were analyzed but specific numerical breakdowns per model were not explicitly listed in the text chunks provided, though Table 6 is referenced. DLNN required minimal processing time (234 s). Validation was performed using 10-fold cross-validation with 95% confidence limits.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Learning based Neural Networks (DLNN), Hybrid Adaboost-Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS), and Hybrid-AdaBoost
architecture_used.input_size: 15 input features (normalized statistical and shape features derived from segmented images)

DLNN.structure: 15 input neurons, 3 hidden layers, 2 output neurons
DLNN.hidden_layer_configuration: Monotonically decreasing number of neurons (m1 ≥ m2 ≥ m3 ... ≥ mn)
DLNN.training_method: Optimization routine applied to network; trained as equal to input dataset rather than target output

ANN.type: Back propagation learning Artificial Neural Network

ANFIS.type: Mamdani based Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System
ANFIS.membership_functions: Triangular membership functions with three linguistic levels (Low, Medium, High)

Hybrid-AdaBoost.components: Combines weak classifiers (including SVM) with weight updates based on error rate

training_details.normalization: Normalization based on mean and standard deviation (Z = (X - μ) / σ)

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for contrast enhancement
Median filtering (11 × 11 kernel) for noise removal and smoothing ragged edges
Local-global block analysis for normalization to remove variable illumination
Normalization based on mean and standard deviation (Z = (X - μ) / σ) for feature normalization
conclusions: The proposed Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system, utilizing Normalized Otsu’s Segmentation (NOS) and various machine learning algorithms, achieved a high classification accuracy of 93% on a dataset of 992 dermoscopy images.
Deep Learning based Neural Networks (DLNN) provided the highest classification accuracy (92.89%) among the tested algorithms and required minimal processing time (234 seconds).

The Hybrid Adaboost-Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm achieved a classification accuracy of 91.7%, which was higher than the standard Gentle AdaBoost (89.1%), Real AdaBoost, and Modest AdaBoost variants.
The Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS) achieved a classification accuracy of 90.3% using 815 derived rules.

The combination of CLAHE and median filtering for preprocessing, along with the Normalized Otsu’s Segmentation algorithm, effectively helped to acquire the affected skin lesion region exactly, overcoming variable illumination issues.
Combining SVM with AdaBoost (Hybrid-AdaBoost) provided better classification accuracy compared to other supervised machine learning algorithms tested in the study.
DLNN provides good classification accuracy even with a smaller amount of dataset compared to traditional Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), which require numerous datasets for training.
The proposed CAD system can assist dermatologists to confirm the diagnosis of melanoma and avoid unnecessary excisional biopsies.
important_remarks: The dataset of 992 images was aggregated from multiple online repositories (BC Cancer Agency, American Cancer Society, DermNet NZ, Meddean Luc.edu) rather than a single standardized benchmark, potentially introducing heterogeneity in image acquisition protocols and lesion characteristics.
The study does not report sensitivity and specificity values for individual models, limiting the ability to assess the trade-off between false positives and false negatives for clinical deployment.

The paper acknowledges a potential data-overfitting problem due to the use of a relatively small dataset (992 samples) and a large number of decision parameters, suggesting that classification accuracy could be improved by adjusting training/testing/validation splits.
The authors note that representing datasets as tensors instead of vectors is a future work item, indicating a limitation in the current feature representation methodology.

The proposed CAD system is validated using Chi-square distribution with a 5% level of significance and 10-fold cross-validation with 95% confidence limits, but external validation on independent, unseen datasets is not reported.

The system relies on extracted statistical and shape features from segmented images; performance is dependent on the accuracy of the Normalized Otsu’s Segmentation (NOS) algorithm, and errors in segmentation could propagate to classification.

The study claims DLNN performs well with a smaller dataset compared to traditional ANN, but this claim is based on internal comparison within the same limited dataset rather than external benchmarking.
